title: Insert and Back Again
subtitle: i to type, Esc to come home
summary: Press i to enter Insert mode and type. Press Esc to return to Normal. Build the Esc reflex.

blocks.text: You are in Normal mode. The cursor sits on a character. Keys do things — they are commands. You want to type some text. How?
Press {key:i}. The status line shows `-- INSERT --`. Now keys do what you'd expect: they type characters.
Type whatever you want. Newlines work. Backspace works. This is just typing.
{key:Esc} returns you to Normal mode. The status line empties. Keys are commands again. {key:Ctrl-[} does the same thing — handy if your {key:Esc} key is far away.

Many remappers swap {key:Caps Lock} and {key:Esc} (or map {key:Caps Lock} to {key:Esc}). On many laptops, {key:Esc} is a tiny touch-strip key — moving it to {key:Caps Lock} is a real productivity win and easy to do in OS settings (no Vim config needed).

Build a reflex: **after every thought, press {key:Esc}.** Finished a sentence? Esc. Finished a function name? Esc. Done with that loop body? Esc. Normal mode is your resting state, not Insert. The minute you finish typing, get back to where you can move.
This sounds annoying. After a week, you stop noticing. After a month, you no longer remember what mode you are in — you just know which command to issue, and the right mode shows up.

blocks.title: What Insert Mode Is Buffering
blocks.text:
From the moment you press {key:i} until the moment you press {key:Esc}, Vim is recording the text you type into a small buffer. When you leave Insert, three things happen:

  1. The text goes into the buffer (you see it).
  2. The text is also stored in the `".` register — accessible as the *last-inserted-text* register, useful in mappings and for the {key:.} repeat command.
  3. If a count was applied (e.g. {key:3}{key:i}{key:hi}{key:Esc}), the buffered text is replayed `count - 1` more times so the final result is `hihihi`.

This is also what {key:.} (dot, repeat last change) replays for an Insert-mode change: not the keystrokes you pressed, but the *text* that ended up in the buffer.

Special keys pressed during Insert ({key:Ctrl-W}, {key:Ctrl-U}, {key:Ctrl-T}, {key:Ctrl-D}, {key:Backspace}, {key:Enter}) are also buffered and replayed faithfully. Insert is not just "typed text" — it is a recording of an editing action that happens to mostly be typing.

rows: Return to Normal mode (cancels pending state too — but does not run autocmds; usually use {key:Esc})

blocks.text: Five more ways into Insert come in the next part: {key:a}, {key:I}, {key:A}, {key:o}, {key:O}. Each saves you a motion. For now, {key:i} is enough.
